title: Non-Bloch band theory of nonlinear eigenvalue problems
abstract: Nonlinear eigenvalue problems arise in a wide range of physical systems, in which system parameters depend on the eigenvalue. Such systems have been proposed to exhibit an extreme sensitivity of their spectra to boundary conditions, which leads to the breakdown of conventional topological characterizations. In this work, we establish a non-Bloch framework for calculating continuum bands that reproduce the spectra of the nonlinear system with open boundary conditions. This non-Bloch band theory enables us not only to calculate the eigenvalues but also to reveal phenomena unique to the nonlinear system. We further investigate the topological bulk-boundary correspondence in a nonlinear Chern insulator within an extended version of this framework.
summary: This paper introduces a non-Bloch band theory specifically designed for nonlinear eigenvalue problems. It addresses the failure of standard Bloch theory in systems where parameters depend on the eigenvalue, which typically causes a discrepancy between periodic and open boundary spectra. By using Generalized Brillouin Zones, the authors can accurately predict the spectra and the emergence of the nonlinear skin effect. The framework is successfully extended to 2D to establish a bulk-boundary correspondence for nonlinear Chern insulators.
main_problem: The breakdown of conventional Bloch band theory and topological characterization in nonlinear eigenvalue problems due to extreme sensitivity to boundary conditions.
main_result: The establishment of a non-Bloch band theory framework that uses Generalized Brillouin Zones (GBZs) to accurately reproduce open boundary condition spectra and predict topological edge states in nonlinear systems.
method: Development of a non-Bloch framework using complex-valued Bloch wavenumbers and the construction of Generalized Brillouin Zones (GBZs) via the characteristic equation of the nonlinear system.
model_or_system: 1D and 2D tight-binding models featuring nonlinear eigenvalue equations where system parameters depend on the eigenvalue, including nonreciprocal hopping models and nonlinear Chern insulators.
key_observables: Complex eigenvalue spectra, winding numbers, Chern numbers, and the biorthogonal inverse participation ratio (bi-IPR).
important_parameters: Nonreciprocity strength (tL/tR, gamma_x), nonlinearity strength (delta), and mass parameter (m).
main_assumptions: The nonlinearity follows specific analytical forms (e.g., f(omega) = omega or -omega^2), and the 2D extension assumes mirror symmetry to suppress skin effects in one direction.
figures_summary: Fig 1 compares Bloch vs. non-Bloch bands and PBC vs. OBC spectra; Fig 3 shows the reproduction of bulk and edge spectra in 2D, the disappearance of edge states, and the calculation of Chern number transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear eigenvalue sensitivity, develops the 1D non-Bloch framework and GBZ construction, investigates the nonlinearity-induced skin effect and pseudo-Hermiticity, and extends the theory to 2D topological Chern insulators.
why_interesting: This work provides a critical theoretical tool for understanding nonlinear dynamics in optics, mechanical oscillators, and weakly interacting bosonic systems where boundary-induced spectral shifts are prevalent.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Eigenvalues of the nonreciprocal hopping system. The black and gray curves represent the non-Bloch and Bloch bands, respectively. The red and orange dots indicate the spectra of the open-boundary and periodic-boundary systems with L = 10, respectively. The parameters are set to be tR = 1 and tL = 0.5.

FIG. 2. Eigenvalues, generalized Brillouin zones, and eigen- states of the model with the nonlinear pseudo-Hermiticity. (a) Bloch and non-Bloch bands. The winding number (15) takes +1, −1, and 0 in the red, blue, and white regions, re- spectively. (b) Spectra of the open-boundary and periodic- boundary systems. (c) Generalized Brillouin zones. The gray curve represents the unit circle. (d) Spatial profiles of the eigenstates defined in Eq. (28). The corresponding eigenval- ues are shown in the same colors in (b). The parameters are m = 0.9, t = 1, and v = 0.5, and the system size is L = 50 in (b) and (d).

FIG. 3. Eigenvalues and Chern number of the nonlinear Chern insulator with nonreciprocal hopping amplitudes. (a) Continuum bands with m = 1. (b) Spectra with m = 1 un- der a full open boundary condition. The gray and red points represent the spectra of the bulk eigenstates and the edge states, respectively. (c) and (d) Spectra under the cylindrical geometry (open boundary conditions in the x direction). The color bar means the biorthogonal inverse participation ratio defined in Eq. (46). (e) Generalized Brillouin zones of the auxiliary bands n = + and n = −. (f) Chern number (50) for the lower band n = −. The Chern number is −1 and 0 in the gray and white regions, respectively.
